---
title: Real-World Example — Six Munich Open Datasets
---

The best way to understand the framework is through the six Munich examples included in this project:

- traffic accidents,
- airport traffic,
- cycling counters,
- EV registrations,
- labor indicators,
- tourism metrics.

## Common end-to-end pattern

Each example follows the same architecture:

1. load external public source files,
2. validate/shape data with `@schema` and Polars transforms,
3. persist curated Delta tables via `@incremental`,
4. build visual analysis in Quarto + Altair.

This repeatable flow is exactly what you want in production data warehousing: one consistent contract from raw ingestion to analytical consumption.

## Snapshot-replacement vs append-only inputs

The examples are especially useful because they include snapshot-style datasets (where suppliers republish full files) and naturally additive series.

With watermarking and merge semantics, both source behaviors can be handled without rewriting pipeline architecture.

## Cloud storage in practice

Examples are configured to read/write Delta tables using environment-driven bucket paths. This keeps notebooks portable across local runs, CI, and cloud storage backends.

## Lessons learned from the collection

Across the six datasets, a few operational lessons repeat:

- choose partition columns based on query filters, not source shape,
- allow controlled schema evolution where source owners change formats,
- keep reset workflows explicit for backfills and historical corrections,
- and apply maintenance regularly to preserve read performance.

## Where to go next

After walking through these examples, a natural next step is to replicate the same pattern on your own domain data:

- land raw files in a stable path,
- codify transform + schema contracts,
- select SCD strategy per entity,
- and automate maintenance as part of platform operations.
